item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been wanting to find this one again. How did I never upvote it before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anything, this skip just shows how unnecessary "Archon" is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would this anomaly count as Archon now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait so is the guy out now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is bogged by the execution. The text is obtuse in a way that I get what's going on, but getting from point A to point B is a slog through various little details that don't contribute to the narrative. This could be so much snappier, but it just isn't.

That aside, "SCP-1474 is to be surrounded by at least 400 individual garlands composed of common daisies (Bellis perennis). Each individual garland must be composed of no fewer than 1080 flowers." is never explained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is my favorite anomalous art piece. It has a simple but meaningful message, and its anomalous effects/Foundation containment make a lot of sense.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote, as a Chinese reading this skip is just like reading meaningless memes.
This works in so many ways. The stuff they used to do to contain it is hilarious. The concept is amazing. The formatting? Meh, but it's comprehensible enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a lot SCPs that cannot be containted, but this is the first one I see that SHOULDN'T be contained. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This scip is more than a description, it's an idea, and that idea is beautiful

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for an explanatory note whose existence immediately makes sense in-universe, and an effect that non-anomalously causes the Foundation to read the note and factor it into their stance on the object.

I want to recommend Dr. Lomax to analyze/criticize more SCP items now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it. It's bizarre and inexplicable, yet it still feels like it has a purpose, we just haven't figured it out yet. I also like the way the foundation managed to think outside the box (no pun intended) and find a way to succesfully contain it in a way that didn't seem obvious at first. It really builds the idea of the foundation genuinely researching these things and finding ways to make them non-threatening instead of just guarding them.
I also like the fact that it was found in a public space, a possible implication being that someone else found it and figured it out before the foundation did Here, have an upvote.

Also, surprised (but not necessarily disappointed) that this not an Are We Cool Yet? thing, or if it, it's not explicit. Not that I have anything against AWCY, but it's nice to see artistic SCPs that exist outside their sphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…oh. oooooooooooooh, this is clever…

of course i wouldn't be the first one to make journey to the west references in an article.

that aside, though, this is really great. the paradox of the magic stopping once you take it outside the bars is fantastic on, like, three different levels. enthusiastically +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You almost lost me with the massive changes to containment every three days. And then you won be back with a quick-witted researcher realizing the connection to imprisonment.

+1. Sassy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The simplest method of containment for many objects would be "dump it in a hole and cover it in cement." In-universe, this isn't generally done because if a containment procedure works well-ishly, then trying to mess with it is a bad idea. Out-of-universe, this isn't done because it's uninteresting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait… I must be missing something, because prior to the uncontainment, couldn't it just be, I dunno, dumped in a hole and covered in cement? I mean, unless it breaks conservation of mass, that'd be the simplest method. (and if it does, it should be noted.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.
Also, abusing the box test to make it keter amuses me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The name "Xiu Lidao" was made by combining the names of two CCP apparatchiks I found on Wikipedia/I thought it sounded like a good name. No deeper meaning.

e: as for Zhang San, fix'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You spelled "Zhang San " wrong,sir.

And no one noticed that"Xiu Lidao"might be "袖里刀"or"修理刀"？Just a kinfe,for repairing things or hidding in sleeve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is now my favorite SCP, and the first one to earn its own exclusive bookmark. It is absolutely amazing.

+1, because this needs to go over 9000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, 1845 already produced a list of how to kill 682 with various destroyed SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for brilliant Dr. Lomax

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the foundation's best "D'oh!" moment (except the day they learn to kill 682 with various easily obtainable and harmless objects). The fact that containing it was what caused it to screw everything over was brilliant. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful. The whole idea about 1474 adapting to its surroundings and how the best way to contain it is exactly NOT containing it blows my mind.

Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it that much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While placing it outside is a "containment" procedure from the Foundations point of view, being outside is not a form of containment from the point of view of the object.

Consider the backstory of the article - it was created as a protest against confinement by someone who is a political prisoner. You could argue that while it is free from physical containment, it is not free from ideological confinement. But let's not get into a recursive argument.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking gorgeous. I upvoted, downvoted, upvoted, downvoted and upvoted again because this thing deserves at least 3 upvotes, and I refuse to let the fact that it can't have them stop me from giving them to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a thought I had: If your containment procedure is to not contain it, doesn't that mean not containing it is containment, making it liable to try to somehow escape from… what, freedom? Making itself contained, thus restarting the entire process?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay, after a bit more than a day, this is now my highest-ranked article! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Despite similar attributes to lackluster AWCY items (art piece what kills you with a cryptic note from the artist), the way they're applied here works pretty well. Also, I love the ridiculous containment procedures the item spews out. They're both entertaining and add a lot to the message. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It passes the locked room test.

Neat, now I have four karma dealies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done,
one question though, why was it Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reminds me a lot of installations I've seen at galleries. It might not be your kind of art, but it appears to be intended as art. It resembles some of the gallery art I've seen.

The only really anomalous part is where all of the sealant comes from. 1st law of thermoD - gone and broken. Well, that and the fact that it can sense its surroundings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good concept, well written. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, rendering an SCP safe by not containing it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I apologize then. (I've been seeing that kind of critique a lot lately, and it's been bothering me.)

As to your intended point, I'm not going to try to argue you into believing that it is art, but is it okay if I sit over here and give you a puzzled look? I'll be as unobtrusive about it as I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing also involves presumably non-clockwork refrigerators, police tape, and water coolers.

What qualities define an art installation? If you mean visually, the picture was taken from a real-life art installation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Misunderstanding me. Obviously, this is nothing like 914 in any significant way except appearance. In that they're both made of clockwork. This is indeed very original; I'm not suggesting otherwise. I'm just saying that I don't buy the object as an art installation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) The artist(s) who made it was/were unaware of the Foundation's existence. They assumed that it would be handled by the Chinese government. Besides, much like a certain verboten art collective, it's not so much who gets hurt as the statement that's of primary concern.
2) Not even a little. It's not tied to the fate of any one prisoner; it's a statement on the absurd and damaging nature of containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) If this thing was designed to protest the imprisonment of a political prisoner, I'd think that it would be designed to try to not cause any harm to other political prisoners. But many of the D-class available in China would likely be political prisoners.

2) Did the Foundation not even attempt to pressure the Chinese government into releasing the prisoner?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's apparently the newest thing all of a sudden. If you can think of any characteristics a new SCP shares with one of the old ones, then you can completely dismiss the new one. 1474 is a copy of 914. 1173 is 1130. 1326 is 1065 and/or 887.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this similar to 914 in any way other than involving gears? Also, how is it not an art installation? It's a three dimensional work designed to express a concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is, I don't see how the thing is an art installation. If it's just 914's little brother, that's not much of a piece of art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed, but not like with the spine of the cosmos, which was changed to a mere commodity by becoming a hat. Here, the correct context was used to neutralize the device.

The piece must be free. If it is caged and hidden, it fights back with ridiculous demands. If displayed, it is benign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I wish I could +1 this comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"During operation, component pieces of SCP1474-01 become impossible to break or harm."

Challenge accepted?

Nevertheless, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So changing the context neutralized the device…..

… WEAR IT LIKE A HAAAAAAATTTTTTTTTT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. The changing containment procedures, the accord reached in the notes at the end; fantastic.

Women xie xiang xianzai ma?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, you posted it. Awesome :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Credit is due to Jean Tinguely, whose Homage to New York serves as inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took forever to get to the payoff, but the payoff is worth it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. "Oh my god, I DON'T KNOW WHAT KIND OF AERSOLIZING AGENT WAS USED OH CHRIST!"

Hopefully the sentence is clearer now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that it's an art/protest piece without any notable, overt, or tacked on questions about whether we are or are not cool, yet or otherwise. Other than the grammar issue Hyaenaboy brought up, I think it's a solid piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I really like the idea of an object being able to change its own ridiculous containment procedures. However:

SCP-1474-02 activates an average of once every two (2) weeks. When active, it displays a announces a new series of containment procedures in Mandarin Chinese necessary to maintain the passive state of SCP-1474-01.

You have some grammar problems here, and I don't want to change them because I don't know what's going on.

liquid polythene, melamine formaldehyde, and [DATA EXPUNGED]

Your article is yet another victim of the computer virus that eats the last item of every list in SCP write-ups.

All and all, okay. I want to vote this up, but it's not quite up to my standard yet.
